summary: Build practical security skills across networking, cryptography, Linux, and Windows by learning the mental models attackers use — and the telemetry defenders rely on.

modules.title: The Protocol Feature Mental Model
modules.strata_theme: Theme 1: Protocol Features Are the Attack Surface
modules.description: Every protocol's usability feature encodes a trust assumption that attackers exploit. Master this pattern once and you can reason about attacks on protocols you've never studied.
objectives: Derive potential attack vectors for any protocol by identifying its trusted assumptions
Identify the specific protocol feature exploited in TCP SYN floods, ARP spoofing, and HTTP header injection

lessons.title: How Protocol Features Become Attack Surfaces
lessons.description: The universal pattern: every protocol's usability assumption is an attacker's entry point. Why TCP, ARP, HTTP, and DNS all follow the same structural logic.

lessons.title: TCP, ARP, and HTTP: Walking Protocols to Their Attacks
lessons.description: Apply the mental model: start from each protocol's spec and derive the attacks directly from the design decisions — not from a list of CVEs.

modules.title: DNS — Single Protocol, Every Domain
modules.strata_theme: Theme 8: DNS Is a Universal Attack and Defense Vector
modules.description: DNS appears in networking attacks, C2 infrastructure, certificate security, and Windows lateral movement detection. DNS literacy is a prerequisite for everything else.
objectives: Describe three ways attackers weaponize DNS: cache poisoning, tunneling for C2, and DGA for resilient infrastructure
Apply DNS records (CAA, SPF, Sysmon Event 22 logging) to reduce attacker leverage

lessons.title: DNS Architecture and How Attackers Abuse It
lessons.description: The recursive resolver trust chain, Kaminsky-style cache poisoning, zone transfer leakage, and DNS as the dominant C2 channel.

lessons.title: Using DNS Records as a Defender
lessons.description: CAA records, DNSSEC, Sysmon Event 22, split-horizon DNS — converting the protocol that attackers love into a detection tripwire.

modules.title: Symmetric Encryption — From CBC to AEAD
modules.strata_theme: Theme 9: AEAD Cipher Modes Represent the Cryptographic Consensus for Symmetric Encryption
modules.description: Three major TLS attacks (BEAST, Lucky13, POODLE) all exploited the same CBC property. Understanding why AEAD fixed them is the unlock for reading cipher suite documentation intelligently.
objectives: Explain why separating encryption from authentication in CBC mode enabled padding oracle attacks
Select correct cipher suites for TLS 1.2 and TLS 1.3 based on OWASP and NIST guidance

lessons.title: Why CBC Kept Breaking: The Case for AEAD
lessons.description: BEAST, Lucky13, POODLE — each attack exploits the structural separation of encryption from authentication in CBC mode. AEAD collapses both into one operation.

lessons.title: Choosing Cipher Suites in Practice
lessons.description: TLS 1.3 is AEAD-only by spec. TLS 1.2 requires careful manual configuration. The exact OWASP and NIST-approved lists — and the TLS 1.2 vs 1.3 nuance that trips up practitioners.

modules.title: Forward Secrecy, KDFs, and PKI
modules.strata_theme: Theme 3: Forward Secrecy and Ephemeral Keys Are Industry Consensus
modules.description: Three distinct cryptographic problems with the same root cause: long-lived secret material. Ephemeral keys, KDFs, and certificate chain validation each solve a different aspect.
objectives: Explain why ephemeral ECDH key exchange prevents retroactive decryption after long-term key compromise
Apply bcrypt or Argon2id with correct parameters for secure password storage
List RFC 5280's four certificate chain validation checks performed by browsers

lessons.title: Ephemeral Keys and Why Forward Secrecy Is Non-Negotiable
lessons.description: Static RSA key exchange meant every recorded TLS session could be decrypted retroactively after key compromise. ECDHE generates independent per-session key material.

lessons.title: Passwords, Hashing, and Certificate Chain Validation
lessons.description: Why bcrypt and Argon2id not SHA-256 for passwords, the bcrypt 72-byte truncation caveat, and how browsers validate certificate chains (RFC 5280 four-check process).

modules.title: Linux Security — Permissions and the Filesystem
modules.strata_theme: Theme 10: Least Privilege Is the Universal Hardening Principle Across All Domains
modules.description: The Linux permission model is both a hardening framework and a forensic map. Knowing where files are supposed to be makes suspicious files findable.
objectives: Use chmod, find -perm, and stat to identify over-privileged files and SUID binaries on a Linux system
Navigate the FHS to locate auth logs, crontabs, temp staging directories, and service configurations during an investigation

lessons.title: The Linux Permission Model for Security Work
lessons.description: rwx triplets, SUID and SGID bits, sudo configuration — and the privilege escalation paths each creates. With the find commands that expose them.

lessons.title: The Filesystem as a Security Map: FHS and Bash Tools
lessons.description: Every security artifact has a predictable location in the FHS. auth.log, crontab, sshd_config, /tmp, /etc/cron.d — and the bash pipelines for investigating each.

modules.title: Persistence — Abusing What the OS Trusts
modules.strata_theme: Theme 6: Persistence Via 'Trusted' OS Mechanisms Is the Attacker's Default
modules.description: Sophisticated attackers don't invent persistence mechanisms — they use cron, systemd, WMI, and registry run keys. Detecting them requires knowing what 'normal' looks like first.
objectives: List five persistence locations used by attackers on Linux and five on Windows, with their legitimate administrative purpose
Detect unauthorized persistence entries by comparing current state against a known-good baseline

lessons.title: How Attackers Hide in Legitimate Autorun Mechanisms
lessons.description: Cron, systemd user services, /tmp staging on Linux; scheduled tasks, WMI subscriptions (filter + consumer + binding), registry HKLM\...\Run keys on Windows. Why each blends into operational noise.

lessons.title: Detecting Persistence Through Baselining
lessons.description: Sysmon Events 12/13 (registry modifications) and Events 19/20/21 (WMI subscriptions). Build the known-good exclusion list, alert on everything else.

modules.title: Windows Telemetry — Seeing the Full Picture
modules.strata_theme: Theme 4: Defense Requires Correlated Multi-Source Telemetry
modules.description: Event 4624, Sysmon, and AD events each cover different attack surfaces and share no single log source. Correlation via ProcessGUID and SessionGUID is what makes detection work.
objectives: Describe the distinct security context provided by Event 4624, Sysmon Events 1/3/10/22, and AD Event 4769
Correlate events across sources using ProcessGUID and SessionGUID to reconstruct a lateral movement chain

lessons.title: Windows Event Sources: What Each Log Provides
lessons.description: Event 4624 tells WHO authenticated. Sysmon tells WHAT ran and what connected. AD events cover DC-level changes. Each has a coverage gap the others fill.

lessons.title: Correlating Events to Detect Lateral Movement
lessons.description: ProcessGUID links process creation to network connections. SessionGUID links that to logon events. AD Event 4769 confirms Kerberos. The seven security-critical registry areas and how they appear in Sysmon.

modules.title: Authentication Weaknesses — NTLM to Kerberoasting
modules.strata_theme: Theme 7: NTLM Is the Cryptographic Weak Link in Windows Authentication
modules.description: NTLM's RC4-based cryptographic weakness underlies Pass-the-Hash and Kerberoasting — the two most common post-exploitation techniques in Active Directory environments.
objectives: Describe the Pass-the-Hash and Kerberoasting attack chains and their specific detection event signatures
Apply the paired-detection principle: identify the monitoring control that must accompany each preventative control

lessons.title: NTLM, Pass-the-Hash, and Kerberoasting
lessons.description: LmPackageName=NTLM in Event 4624, TicketEncryptionType=0x17 in Event 4769, Event 4662 for DCSync — the exact attack chains and the exact detection signatures.

lessons.title: Layered Controls and the Bypass-Always Pattern
lessons.description: Every preventative control has a documented bypass — execution policy, ARP validation, NTLM. Effective defence means paired detection for each control, not just stacking more controls.
